schools.summary: Matthew Henry preaches on how sin lessens a man: Reuben forfeited the birthright by defiling his father's bed, yet was degraded, not disinherited; the double portion went to Joseph, the dominion to Judah and the Messiah.
Clarke and Wesley: Reuben lost the birthright by his transgression (Gen. 35:22; 49:4), the precedency passing to Judah; 'sons of Israel' is emphatic, the register ruled by privilege, not birth.
Ellicott reads the parenthesis as a defence of the Davidic monarchy; the birthright given 'to Joseph' (some MSS), the double portion defined by the law of Deut. 21:15-17.
Gill marks the structure: the list proper begins at v.3, all between explaining the forfeited birthright.

themes: double portion to Joseph, dominion to Judah

schools.summary: Keil marks v.2 as the parenthesis before the sons are enumerated in v.3; Ellicott reads 'prevailed' as 'was mighty,' the chief ruler David (1 Sam. 13:14) and ultimately Christ (Heb. 7:14).
Clarke gives the Syriac, Arabic, and Targum reading of the chief ruler as the Messiah; Wesley: not the person but the tribe of Judah prevailed.
Gill: Judah excelled in number, valour, and dignity, so the genealogy follows dominion, not birthright.

themes: dominion to Judah, birthright to Joseph

schools.summary: Ellicott: the four sons answer exactly to Gen. 46:9; Exod. 6:14; Num. 26:5-7, and notes Hezron and Carmi curiously recurring among the clans of Judah.
Gill anchors the four names to the older Mosaic record, the Chronicler adding nothing.
Gill traces the princely line through Joel (son of Hanoch or Carmi) down to Beerah and the captivity under Tiglath-pileser, admitting we know no more than the names.
Ellicott gives the Septuagint's slightly altered arrangement of the pedigree.
Clarke: the ten tribes kept princes of their own until Tiglath-pileser; with Beerah that dominion ended, and the Targum makes him Baruch.
Gill rejects the tradition that Beerah was a prophet and the father of Hosea: name, title, time, and tribe all disagree.
Ellicott identifies the chiefs Jeiel, Zechariah, and Bela as heads of the four Reubenite clans of v.3, matching the muster of Num. 26:7 and the method of Num. 2:34.
Gill leaves open whether 'his brethren' means Beerah's own brothers or the rest of Reuben's posterity.

Ellicott sites Aroer, Nebo, and Baal-meon (all named on the Stone of Mesha) as the borders of Bela's holding, and cautions that omitted links leave Bela and Beerah possibly contemporary; Keil ties the spread to the herds multiplying in Gilead.
Gill traces Bela's pedigree to Joel through Shema, whom Kimchi equates with Shemaiah.
Clarke sets Aroer on the Arnon and Nebo as both city and mountain, all beyond Jordan.

Ellicott: the Reubenites spread eastward to the desert as their herds multiplied; the phrase 'from the river Euphrates' hints the record was written in Babylonia, the desert Ezra took four months to cross.
Wesley: their border reached toward the Euphrates through the wilderness of Kedemoth but never to the river itself.
Gill leaves the subject open — Bela himself or the tribe of Reuben.
Keil and Ellicott refer the war to Bela's clan specifically, fighting the Hagarites (the Hagrim of Ps. 83:7, named in Assyrian records) for the pasture east of Gilead.
Gill reads it of the Reubenites with Gad and half-Manasseh; the Hagarites are Ishmaelites of Hagar, not the Targum's 'Hungarians.'
Clarke: the tent-dwelling nomad Hagarites were extirpated and their herds and tents seized.

debates.question: Who waged the war of v.10 against the Hagarites?

sides.position: Bela and his clan, whose eastward spread had just been described
the tribe of Reuben together with Gad and half-Manasseh

themes: Bela's clan or the whole tribe

schools.summary: Keil maps Gad's holding: Bashan in its wider sense (Og's realm north of the Jabbok) unto Salcah (modern Szalchad) on the eastern boundary.
Gill: the Gadites dwelt beside the Reubenites, holding one part of Gilead.
Keil explains the chiefs, not the sons of Gad (Gen. 46:16), are listed because the genealogical link was lost; four heads dwell 'in Bashan,' seven more follow in v.13.
Gill: Joel the chief of Gad, distinct from the Joel of Reuben.
Clarke reports the Targum's anachronistic titles: prince of the Sanhedrin, master of the college, judges in Mathnan.
Keil: the seven named are the brethren of the chiefs, by their fathers'-houses, their pedigree following in vv.14-15.
Gill: men of eminence and note beside the four chiefs.
Clarke notes the verse is wanting in the Syriac and Arabic.
Gill traces the seven up from Abihail through Huri, Jaroah, Gilead, Michael, Jeshishai, and Jahdo to Buz.
Henry notes the pedigree ascends from son to father here, where Reuben's in v.4 descended.
Keil dates the head Ahi, son of Abdiel, to the days of Jotham or Jeroboam, when the Gadite registers were drawn up.
Gill: Ahi the principal man of these families, each of the others also a head of his house.
Keil sets Ahi as head of the fathers'-houses, living in the days of Jotham or Jeroboam when the registers were made (v.17).
Keil: Gad dwelt in Gilead south of the Jabbok, in Bashan and her villages, and in the pasture-lands of Sharon — which he takes to be the famous coastal plain from Carmel to Joppa.
Clarke distinguishes three places named Sharon and assigns Gad's to a district in Bashan beyond Jordan (Josh. 12:18).
Gill: they dwelt in the part of Gilead belonging to Gad.

sides.position: the famous coastal plain of Sharon, from Carmel to Joppa
a separate trans-Jordan Sharon, a district in Bashan

schools.summary: Keil: the two kings did not reign together, so two registrations are meant; Jotham's is named first for Judah's legitimacy, Jeroboam's after he restored Israel's borders (2 Kings 14:25).
Henry: they prided themselves on their pedigrees as 'children of the covenant' on the eve of ruin, yet the record may be a pledge that though cast out they were not cast off for ever.
Gill: all the foregoing were reckoned by these registers.

Henry makes the 44,760-strong host a lesson: as the seed of believing Abraham they trusted not in their army but in God, and as the seed of praying Jacob cried to him in the battle (Deut. 20:15; 2 Chron. 13:14).
Keil: the account stands here, though Manasseh shares the war, because of the character of the Chronicler's sources, not artificial symmetry.
Gill: the tribes joined their forces as one across Jordan.

themes: the army of the trans-Jordan tribes
the seed of Abraham and Jacob

schools.summary: Keil identifies the Hagarites, Jetur, Nephish, and Nodab as Arab and Ishmaelite desert tribes, and dates this war later than that of v.10, before the Assyrian exile.
Clarke takes this to be probably the same war as v.10, and notes the Targum's 'Hongarites.'
Gill refers the Hagarites back to those before mentioned.

debates.question: Is this the same war as the Hagarite war of v.10?

sides.position: a distinct, later war, connected only by similar causes
probably the same war already mentioned in v.10

schools.summary: Keil: 'it was helped to them' by God; the language shows a life-or-death struggle over pasture that became a war of extermination.
Wesley: they were helped by God, who gave courage and success; Clarke notes the Targum's 'trusted in his Word.'
Gill: helped either by their brethren west of Jordan or rather by the Lord himself.

themes: victory of God, not of numbers

schools.summary: Keil dwells on the round-number booty (50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep), comparing the Midianite spoil of Num. 31.
Clarke: a war of extermination justified only by God's direction against a people whose cup of idolatry was full.
Gill: they took the cattle from the enemy's camp and fields.
Keil: the war 'was of God,' who brought it to this bloody result (cf. 2 Chron. 25:20; 1 Sam. 17:47); they held the land until the Assyrian captivity.
Wesley: the war prospered because undertaken in God's fear and carried on in dependence on him; Clarke thinks the 100,000 were enslaved, not slain.
Gill infers a great enemy army from the many slain.
Keil and Ellicott map Manasseh's spread to Baal-hermon (Baal-gad/Banias), Senir, and Mount Hermon, Senir being the Amorite name of the range (Deut. 3:9).
Henry: their lot was Bashan only, but they increased in wealth and power and spread north to Hermon.
Gill: they dwelt in the Gilead and Bashan Moses assigned, not the conquered land.
Keil counts seven heads of Manasseh, noting a name apparently lost before 'Epher'; Ellicott makes the meagre memorial proof that fuller records once existed.
Gill: these were principal men of the half-tribe.

Ellicott presses the figure of whoredom against Israel's Husband Jehovah, the apostasy showing in high places, star-worship, and divination, the conquest and coming invasion alike God's work (Isa. 10:5-15); Keil: all two-and-a-half tribes fell after the gods of the destroyed nations.
Gill spreads the guilt over Reuben and Gad as well as Manasseh.
Clarke: God gave the idolatrous Hagarites into their hands, and now the same judgment hangs over the idolatrous conquerors.

Ellicott argues Pul and Tiglath-pileser were one king under two names, identifies the places of exile (Habor, Hara as Media, the river of Gozan as the Habur), and notes these are where Sargon later sent the western tribes; Keil takes Pul and Tiglath as two distinct kings.
Gill notes the Targum's reverent 'the Word of the God of Israel.'
Clarke on the 'Tiglath'/'Tilgath' variants, referring the reader to the fuller history in 2 Kings 17-18.

debates.question: Are 'Pul' and 'Tiglath-pileser' two kings or one?

sides.position: one king under two names — Pul his original name, discarded at his accession
two distinct kings — Pul who first attacked, Tiglath-pileser who deported

themes: the deportation to Halah and Gozan

schools.summary: Keil reads the chain 'his son... his son' as the descent from Joel to Beerah, tied to v.7 and the 'generations' formula of Gen. 2:4; Ellicott notes 'Baal' was an innocent element in early Israelite names (Eshbaal, Meribbaal, Beeliada).

themes: the middle links of the princely line
